Rotator cuff tears
Asheesh Bedi 1,2 , Julie Bishop 3 , Jay Keener 4 , Drew A. Lansdown 5 , Ofer Levy 6 , Peter MacDonald 7 , Nicola Maffulli
Joo Han Oh 9 , Vani J. Sabesan 10 , Joaquin Sanchez-Sotelo 11 , Riley J. Williams III 12  & Brian T. Feeley
Abstract
Sections
Rotator cuff tears are the most common upper extremity condition seen
Introduction
by primary care and orthopaedic surgeons, with a spectrum ranging
Epidemiology
from tendinopathy to full-thickness tears with arthritic change. Some
Mechanisms/pathophysiology
tears are traumatic, but most rotator cuff problems are degenerative.
Diagnosis, screening and
Not all tears are symptomatic and not all progress, and many patients
prevention
in whom tears become more extensive do not experience symptom
Management
worsening. Hence, a standard algorithm for managing patients is
Quality of life
challenging. The pathophysiology of rotator cuff tears is complex
Outlook
and encompasses an interplay between the tendon, bone and muscle.
Rotator cuff tears begin as degenerative changes within the tendon,
with matrix disorganization and inflammatory changes. Subsequently,
tears progress to partial-thickness and then full-thickness tears. Muscle
quality, as evidenced by the overall size of the muscle and intramuscular
fatty infiltration, also influences symptoms, tear progression and the
outcomes of surgery. Treatment depends primarily on symptoms, with
non-operative management sufficient for most patients with rotator
cuff problems. Modern arthroscopic repair techniques have improved
recovery, but outcomes are still limited by a lack of understanding of
how to improve tendon to bone healing in many patients.
